sanskrit: शक्नोतीहैव यः सोढुं प्राक् शरीर-विमोक्षणात् । काम-क्रोधोद्भवं वेगं स युक्तः स सुखी नरः ॥
transliteration: śaknotīhaiva yaḥ soḍhuṃ prāk śarīra-vimokṣaṇāt | kāma-krodhodbhavaṃ vegaṃ sa yuktaḥ sa sukhī naraḥ ||
literal_meaning: Whoever is able, even here, before the body is released, to endure the impulse born of desire and anger—he is disciplined, he is the happy man.
contextual_meaning: True psychological freedom is won by withstanding the visceral surge of craving and rage while still embodied; the person who can sit with these impulses without acting them out attains an inner steadiness that is experienced as lasting well-being.
psychological_state: High frustration tolerance; capacity for metacognitive observation of impulses; integration rather than repression of desire and anger.
trigger_scenario: You’re stuck in traffic, late for an important meeting, and another driver cuts you off—your heart races, palms sweat, and you feel the urge to honk or shout. Instead of reacting, you notice the surge, breathe through it, and keep your hands relaxed on the wheel.
anchor_text: Hold steady through desire and anger; inner calm is freedom.
